aliases: Satay with Peanut Sauce and Rice Cake

english: Satay with Peanut Sauce and Rice Cake

food_subgroup: Grilled meat skewers with rice cake and peanut sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a chicken satay entry (FOODON_03311737) but does not specifically represent the combined dish with lontong rice cake. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for sate ayam with lontong as a composite dish. A0CKW (grilled chicken meat) used as closest parent anchor for the primary protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for sate ayam with lontong as a composite dish.

serving.description: 1 plate (approximately 5 skewers with lontong and peanut sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of sate ayam with lontong is approximately 350 g, comprising 5 chicken skewers (about 150 g grilled chicken), 150 g lontong rice cake, and 50 g peanut sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Satay, chicken, with peanut sauce — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo provides nutrient data for chicken satay with peanut sauce. Lontong rice cake component values adapted from HPB Foodo lontong entry. Composite per-100g values derived by weighted average: approximately 43% grilled chicken with peanut sauce and 43% lontong, 14% additional peanut sauce, consistent with standard Singapore hawker serving proportions. Values represent the combined dish as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sate ayam with lontong as a composite dish. GI estimated at 50 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: lontong (compressed boiled rice cake), which is analogous to white rice (GI ~64–72) but with lower GI due to compression and retrogradation of starch during the lontong preparation process. Peer-reviewed evidence (Brand-Miller et al., Diabetes Care 2008) indicates compressed rice cakes have GI in the range 48–55. The protein and fat from chicken and peanut sauce further attenuate the glycemic response. Composite GI estimated at 50 (low category). GL per 350 g serving = (50 × 53.9 g carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 27.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and peanut sauce marinade
hypertension.guidance: Each 350 g serving provides approximately 1085 mg sodium (45% of the 2400 mg daily limit), so individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce, limit peanut sauce portions, and balance with low-sodium foods throughout the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from lontong rice cake carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic load of approximately 27 per standard serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a smaller portion (e.g., half the lontong) to reduce glycemic load to below 20, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from peanut sauce and sodium from soy-based marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The peanut sauce contributes saturated fat (~1.9 g per 100 g) and the soy-based marinade contributes significant sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 200–250 g and choose versions with reduced peanut sauce to keep saturated fat and sodium within cardioprotective targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sate Ayam with Lontong is a popular Southeast Asian dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres and Indonesian restaurants. It consists of tender chicken pieces marinated in a spiced blend of turmeric, lemongrass, galangal, shallots, garlic, and coconut milk, threaded onto bamboo skewers and grilled over charcoal until lightly charred and fragrant. The skewers are served alongside lontong — compressed boiled rice cakes made by packing glutinous or regular rice into banana leaf cylinders and boiling until firm — and a rich, sweet-savoury peanut sauce made from ground roasted peanuts, palm sugar, tamarind, soy sauce, and chilli. The dish delivers a complex interplay of smoky, sweet, nutty, and mildly spicy flavours with contrasting textures: juicy, slightly charred chicken against soft, dense rice cake and creamy peanut sauce. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein (~10 g per 100 g) from chicken, moderate carbohydrate (~15 g per 100 g) from lontong, and moderate fat (~7 g per 100 g) predominantly from peanut sauce. Sodium content is notable (~310 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce in the marinade and peanut sauce. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker settings. Clinically, the glycemic load is moderate (~27 per standard serving), making portion control of the lontong component advisable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk. The dish is a culturally significant staple at Malay and Indonesian food stalls across Singapore and is commonly consumed as a main meal or festive food.
common_user_queries: How many calories in sate ayam with lontong?
Is chicken satay with lontong healthy?
What is sate ayam made of?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo entries for chicken satay with peanut sauce and lontong as separate components, combined using standard serving proportion estimates. No single HPB entry covers the exact composite dish sate ayam with lontong as served. GI is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed compressed rice cake data. Confidence is medium rather than high because the composite per-100g values involve weighted averaging rather than direct measurement of the combined dish.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this dish or its components. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this dish. All other -1 values have been replaced with estimated or source-derived values where available. Composite per-100g nutrient profile derived by weighted average of HPB Foodo chicken satay with peanut sauce (~57% by weight) and lontong (~43% by weight) components, consistent with a standard 350 g hawker serving of 5 skewers with lontong. GI estimated at 50 (low category) based on analogous compressed rice cake data from University of Sydney GI Database and Brand-Miller et al. (2008), with attenuation from protein and fat co-ingestion. Sodium is moderate-to-high due to soy sauce in marinade and peanut sauce; individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size. The dish is widely available in Singapore hawker centres and Indonesian restaurants. Halal status is typical for hawker stall preparations but should be verified per vendor.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed composite per-100g nutrient profile from HPB Singapore Foodo chicken satay and lontong component data using weighted average.
Added all mandatory multilingual local names: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Populated aliases_by_language with Tamil and Hindi entries.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 and selenium_mcg = -1 with explicit source-gap justification in notes (G3 sentinel value rule).
Set GI to 50 (numeric > 0) with analogous estimate basis from University of Sydney GI Database / Brand-Miller et al. 2008.
Set ontology match types to closest_parent or none only — no invented codes, no disallowed match_type strings.
Included specific clinical guidance for Type 2 Diabetes with human_condition_name set to 'Type 2 Diabetes'.
Corrected the Hindi alias in `aliases_by_language` from mixed-script ('सते अयाம लोंटोंग के साथ' with Tamil 'ம') to pure Devanagari ('सते अयाम लोंटोंग के साथ' with Devanagari 'म').
Updated the flat `aliases` list to include the corrected pure Devanagari Hindi alias and removed the mixed-script version, ensuring perfect consistency between `aliases_by_language` and `aliases`.
